poems.title: Seviyorum Seni
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet
lines: ağzımı dayayıp musluğa su içer gibi,
denizi ilk defa uçakla geçer gibi,

poems.title: Yaşamaya Dair
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet
lines: yani bütün işin gücün yaşamak olacak.
hem de yüzünü bile görmediğin insanlar için,
hem de hiç kimse seni buna zorlamamışken,
hem de en güzel en gerçek şeyin
Yani, öylesine ciddiye alacaksın ki yaşamayı,
hem de öyle çocuklara falan kalır diye değil,

poems.title: Davet
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet
lines: Akdeniz'e bir kısrak başı gibi uzanan
ve ipek bir halıya benzeyen toprak,
Yaşamak bir ağaç gibi tek ve hür

poems.title: Salkımsöğüt
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet
lines: Rüzgâr kanatlı atlılar gibi geçti hayat!

poems.title: Avondliedeke III
poems.author: Alice Nahon
lines: 't Is goed in 't eigen hart te kijken
Nog even vóór het slapen gaan,
Of ik van dageraad tot avond
Geen enkel hart heb zeer gedaan;
Of ik geen ogen heb doen schreien,
Geen weemoed op een wezen lei;
En vind ik, in het huis mijns harten,
Dat ik mijn armen heb gewonden
Rondom één hoofd, dat eenzaam was...;
Dan voel ik, op mijn jonge lippen,
't Is goed in 't eigen hart te kijken
En zó z'n ogen toe te doen.

poems.title: Mensenogen
poems.author: Alice Nahon
lines: Ik houd van ogen door weemoed gewijd
Ik houd van ogen die hebben geschreid
Die hunkerend uitzien van groot gemis
Ik houd van ogen die prachtig spontaan
Van grote smart naar geluk willen gaan
Die moe van gemijmer in avond'lijk land
Weer blikkeren uit ruiten waar zonlicht in brandt.
Maar God'lijk de kijkers die schreiens gereed
Schitteren en zingen hoog boven hun leed
't Zijn zij die der zielen ellende bevroên
die lachen om and'ren geen zeer te doen.

poems.title: Moederken
poems.author: Guido Gezelle
lines: 't en zij dat beeld in mij,
o Moge ik, u onweerdig, nooit
maar eerzaam laat ze leven in mij,

poems.title: Het Mooiste Gedicht
poems.author: Maurits Tachelet
lines: En is juist daarom zo onsterfelijk

poems.title: Voor Altijd
poems.author: Anais Heyde
lines: mijn verdriet en mijn vreugde lezen
was jij het die mij verder droeg
was jij het waar 'k de weg aan vroeg

poems.title: Ik Heb Je Lief
poems.author: Stef Bos
lines: Ik zie hoe landen zich verscheuren
Ik voel de kanker van cynisme
Ik zie de mensen zonder dromen
Jaagt de haat door oude straten
Maar ik, ik heb een medicijn
Ik heb je lief... Ik heb je liever
Ik heb je lief... Ik heb je liever
Dan kon ik dichter bij je zijn
Dan sliep ik in jouw hart
En ik woonde in jouw hoofd

poems.title: Berceuse Nr. 2
lines: slaap als een reus van een roos
doe de deur dicht van de doos

poems.title: Five Lines
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet (trans.)
lines: in the heart, in the streets, in the books
from the lullabies of the mothers
to the news report that the speaker reads,
understanding, my love, what a great joy it is,
and what is on the way.

poems.title: Regarding Art
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet (trans. Blasing & Konuk)
lines: Sometimes, I, too, tell the ah's of my heart
like the blood-red beads of a ruby rosary
strung on strands of golden hair!
takes to the air on wings made of steel
like the I-beams of my suspension bridges!
I don't pretend the nightingale's lament
to the rose isn't easy on the ears...
But the language that really speaks to me
are Beethoven sonatas played on copper,
You can 'have' galloping off in a cloud of dust!
Me, I wouldn't trade for the purest-bred
the sixth mph of my iron horse
Sometimes my eye is caught like a big dumb fly
in the corners of my room.
But I really look up to
Were I to meet the male beauty
'young Adonis, god of Byblos,' on a bridge,
Though I can smoke third-class cigarettes
I can't roll tobacco - even the finest -
in paper by hand and smoke it!
trade my wife dressed in her
Maybe I don't have a 'poetic soul'?
when I love my own children

poems.title: Ceviz Ağacı
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet
lines: ben bir ceviz ağacıyım Gülhane Parkı'nda,
budak budak, şerham şerham ihtiyar bir ceviz.
Ne sen bunun farkındasın, ne polis farkında.
Ben bir ceviz ağacıyım Gülhane Parkı'nda.
Yapraklarım suda balık gibi kıvıl kıvıl.
Yapraklarım ipek mendil gibi tiril tiril,
Yapraklarım ellerimdir, tam yüz bin elim var.
Yüz bin elle dokunurum sana, İstanbul'a.
Yüz bin gözle seyrederim seni, İstanbul'u.
Yüz bin yürek gibi çarpar, çarpar yapraklarım.
Ben bir ceviz ağacıyım Gülhane Parkı'nda.
Ne sen bunun farkındasın, ne polis farkında.

poems.title: The Walnut Tree
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet (trans.)
lines: I am a walnut tree in Gulhane Park
an old walnut, knot by knot, shred by shred
Neither you are aware of this, nor the police
I am a walnut tree in Gulhane Park
My leaves are nimble, nimble like fish in water
My leaves are sheer, sheer like a silk handkerchief
pick, wipe, my rose, the tear from your eyes
My leaves are my hands, I have one hundred thousand
I touch you with one hundred thousand hands,
My leaves are my eyes, I look in amazement
I watch you with one hundred thousand eyes,
Like one hundred thousand hearts, beat, beat my leaves
I am a walnut tree in Gulhane Park
neither you are aware of this, nor the police

poems.title: Seni Düşünmek
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet
lines: en güzel şarkıyı dinlemek gibi bir şey...

poems.title: Thinking Of You
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet (trans.)
lines: Thinking of you is pretty, hopeful,
It is like listening to the most beautiful song
From the most beautiful voice on earth...
But hope is not enough for me any more,
I don't want to listen to songs any more,

poems.title: Our Eyes
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet (trans.)
lines: Our eyes are limpid drops of water.
a tiny sign of our genius
which has given life to cold iron.
Our eyes are limpid drops of water
the drop in a block of ice
In these eyes filled with limpid
pure tears had failed to emerge
easily as if made of hollow wood.

poems.title: On Living
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet (trans. Blasing & Konuk)
lines: you must live with great seriousness
I mean without looking for something
I mean living must be your whole occupation.
so much so and to such a degree
that, for example, your hands tied behind your back,
in your white coat and safety glasses,
even for people whose faces you've never seen,
is the most real, the most beautiful thing.
I mean, you must take living so seriously
that even at seventy, for example,
and not for your children, either,
but because although you fear death
because living, I mean, weighs heavier.
Let's say you're seriously ill, need surgery -
which is to say we might not get
Even though it's impossible not to feel sad
about going a little too soon,
we'll still laugh at the jokes being told,
we'll look out the window to see it's raining,
Let's say we're at the front -
for something worth fighting for, say.
There, in the first offensive, on that very day,
we might fall on our face, dead.
We'll know this with a curious anger,
but we'll still worry ourselves to death
about the outcome of the war,
and we have eighteen more years, say,
before the iron doors will open.
We'll still live with the outside,
I mean with the outside beyond the walls.
I mean, however and wherever we are,
we must live as if we will never die.
a gilded mote on blue velvet -
I mean this, our great earth.
This earth will grow cold one day,
not like a block of ice
but like an empty walnut it will roll along
You must grieve for this right now
- you have to feel this sorrow now -
for the world must be loved this much
if you're going to say 'I lived'...

poems.title: Letters From A Man In Solitary
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet (trans. Blasing & Konuk)
lines: I carved your name on my watchband
I don't have a pearl-handled jackknife
or a plane tree with its head in the clouds.
Trees may grow in the yard,
but I'm not allowed to see the sky overhead...
How many others are in this place?
they're all together far from me.
To talk anyone besides myself is forbidden.
But I find my conversation so boring,
my dear wife, that I sing songs.
that awful, always off-key voice of mine
touches me so that my heart breaks.
And just like the barefoot orphan
lost in the snow in those old sad stories,
my heart - with moist blue eyes
and a little red runny rose -
wants to snuggle up in your arms.
that right now I'm this weak,
It's spring outside, my dear wife, spring.
the smell of fresh earth, birds singing, etc.
And inside the bed comes alive with bugs,
the water jug no longer freezes,
and in the morning sun floods the concrete...
it comes and goes from me,
And as the day turns to afternoon,
the glass of the barred window catches fire,
And inside this is spring's darkest hour.
In short, the demon called freedom,
with its glittering scales and fiery eyes,
I know this from experience, my dear wife,
Today they took me out in the sun for the first time.
And I just stood there, struck for the first time
in my life by how far away the sky is,
Then I respectfully sat down on the earth.
I leaned back against the wall.
For a moment no trap to fall into,
no struggle, no freedom, no wife.

poems.title: Letter To My Wife
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet (trans. Blasing & Konuk)
lines: my memory will vanish like black smoke in the wind.
Of course you'll live, red-haired lady of my heart:
grief lasts at most a year.
a body swinging from a rope.
My heart can't accept such a death.
if some poor gypsy's hairy black
slips a noose around my neck,
they'll look in vain for fear
In the twilight of my last morning
I will see my friends and you,
and I'll go to my grave
regretting nothing but an unfinished song...
eyes sweeter than honey - my bee!
Why did I write you they want to hang me?
and they don't just pluck a man's head
my sciatica is acting up again.
a prisoner's wife must always think good thoughts.

poems.title: It's This Way
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet (trans. Blasing & Konuk)
lines: I stand in the advancing light,
my hands hungry, the world beautiful.
My eyes can't get enough of the trees -
A sunny road runs through the mulberries,
I'm at the window of the prison infirmary.
being captured is beside the point,
the point is not to surrender.

poems.title: Ben Senden Önce Ölmek İsterim
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet
lines: Ve bir gün yabani bir çiçek

poems.title: I Want To Die Before You
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet (trans.)
lines: I want to die before you.
Do you think that who passes later
and put me on the stove in your room
The jar shall be made of glass,
so that you can see me inside...
I renounced from being part of the earth,
I renounced from being a flower
to be able to stay with you.
will mix with each other so much
our grains will fall side by side.
We will dive into the soil together.
And one day, if a wild flower
feeds from this piece of soil
definitely there will be two flowers:
I don't think of death yet.
I will give birth to a child.
I will live, but long, very long,
But I find our way of funeral rather unlikable.
Until I die, I think this will get better.
Is there a hope you'll get out of prison
A voice in me says: maybe.

poems.title: I Think Of You
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet (trans.)
lines: and I feel the scent of my mother
my mother, the most beautiful of all.
You are on the carousel of the festival inside me
you hover around, your skirt and your hair flying
Mere seconds between finding your beautiful face
like a wound on my heart
what is the reason that I hear your voice
and I can't help getting up with excitement?
I kneel down and look at your hands
I want to touch your hands but I can't
of the drama that I am playing in my twilight.

poems.title: I Love You (English)
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet (trans.)
lines: like dipping bread into salt and eating
Like waking up at night with high fever
and drinking water, with the tap in my mouth
Like unwrapping the heavy box from the postman
with no clue what it is
like flying over the sea in a plane
when it gets dark softly in Istanbul
Like thanking God that we live.

poems.title: Hymn To Life
poems.author: Nâzım Hikmet (trans. Blasing & Konuk)
lines: The hair falling on your forehead
Suddenly something stirred on the ground.
The trees are whispering in the dark.
Your bare arms will be cold.
Far off where we can't see,
But I know a wind comes up with the moon.
Your bare arms will be cold.
from the branches lost in the dark,
Under my hand your bare flesh
is like the fuzzy skin of a fruit.
Neither a song of the heart
before the trees, birds, and insects,
my hand on my wife's flesh
It's the tongue of a leopard at a spring,
My hand is like a seed
Neither a song of the heart
My hand thinking on my wife's flesh
is the hand of the first man.
Like a root that finds water underground,
not to live in order to die
as red female hair blows across my face,
as something stirs on the ground,
as the trees whisper in the dark,
and as the moon rises far off
my hand on my wife's flesh
before the trees, birds, and insects,
I want the right of life,
of the leopard at the spring,
I want the right of the first man.
